name: AOD-9604 (Anti-Obesity Drug 9604)
summary: AOD-9604 is a modified 16-amino-acid fragment of the C-terminus of human growth hormone (Tyr-hGH 177-191), engineered to retain the fat-mobilizing (lipolytic) activity of GH without its effects on IGF-1, blood glucose, or cell growth. It is studied as a fat-loss compound, though human efficacy evidence is limited and mixed. It is categorized here under Growth Hormone because it is a GH-derived fragment, but note it does NOT raise IGF-1 or act on the classic GH receptor.
mechanism:
AOD-9604 corresponds to the lipolytic C-terminal domain of human growth hormone (residues 176-191) with an added N-terminal tyrosine for stability, giving Tyr-hGH 177-191. In preclinical models it stimulates lipolysis (breakdown of stored triglycerides) and inhibits lipogenesis (conversion of food into new fat), reproducing the fat-reducing effect of full GH without its diabetogenic or growth-promoting actions.

Mechanistically, the fat-loss effect is tied to the beta-3 adrenergic receptor (beta3-AR), the principal lipolytic receptor on adipocytes. In obese and beta3-AR knockout mice, Heffernan et al. showed that chronic AOD-9604 reduced body weight and increased beta3-AR expression in fat, but the weight-reducing effect was lost in beta3-AR knockout animals, indicating the receptor is required for the chronic effect even though the peptide does not appear to bind beta3-AR directly. Critically, unlike full-length GH, AOD-9604 does not activate the GH receptor, does not raise serum IGF-1, and does not impair glucose tolerance in human studies.
benefits: Stimulates lipolysis (fat breakdown) and inhibits lipogenesis in preclinical models
Reduces body weight and body fat in obese mice via a beta-3 adrenergic-dependent pathway
Does not raise IGF-1 or impair glucose tolerance, unlike full-length growth hormone
Appetite-neutral in studies (does not act on GLP-1 or central appetite pathways like GLP-1 drugs)
Very good safety/tolerability profile across six human trials (indistinguishable from placebo)
Explored anecdotally for cartilage/joint support, though human evidence is minimal
risks: Human efficacy evidence is limited and MIXED: the largest Phase IIb obesity trial failed to reach statistical significance vs placebo and development was discontinued (2007)
Not FDA-approved as a drug; it holds only GRAS (food-ingredient) recognition and is not an approved obesity therapeutic
Prohibited in sport (WADA prohibited list, S2 peptide hormones/growth factors)
No published dedicated human pharmacokinetic study of the subcutaneous route; SC dosing is extrapolated from oral/IV trial experience and community use
Long-term safety of injected research-grade material and typical fat-loss doses is not clinically established
sideEffects: Injection-site redness, irritation, or soreness (subcutaneous use)
Generally well tolerated; adverse-event rates were indistinguishable from placebo in trials
Occasional mild headache or fatigue reported anecdotally
No consistent effect on appetite, IGF-1, or blood glucose reported

dosage.notes: Common educational protocol is roughly 250-500 mcg subcutaneously once daily; 300 mcg is the most frequently cited figure. Human clinical trials used ORAL tablets (1, 5, 10, 20, 30 mg) with the 1 mg oral dose performing best in the Phase 2 study; injectable SC microgram dosing is a community convention, not a validated clinical regimen. This is educational information, not medical advice.
dosage.timing: Typically dosed once daily in the morning on an empty stomach; the very short half-life means the peptide clears quickly, so consistent daily timing is used rather than titration. Some protocols split into AM/PM doses. Not appetite-mediated, so timing relative to meals is for consistency rather than hunger control.
dosage.maxDose: Community SC use rarely exceeds ~500-600 mcg/day. Human oral trials went as high as 30 mg/day; higher doses did not improve fat loss (the 1 mg oral arm performed best), so escalating dose has no demonstrated benefit. Any ceiling here is anecdotal and not clinically established.

steps.note: 300 mcg subcutaneously once daily, typically AM on an empty stomach. Microgram SC dosing is a community convention extrapolated from oral trial data; human SC efficacy is not clinically validated.

halfLife: Short; reported elimination half-life of roughly 30-40 minutes (~38 min), cleared rapidly
cycleLength: Commonly run in blocks of about 12 weeks, often stacked with diet and exercise; trials evaluated 12-24 week treatment periods

reconstitution.notes: 5 mg vial + 2 mL bacteriostatic water = 2.5 mg/mL (2500 mcg/mL), so 300 mcg = 0.12 mL (12 units on a U-100 insulin syringe) and 500 mcg = 0.2 mL (20 units). Swirl gently to dissolve; do not shake.

storage: Store lyophilized vials frozen or refrigerated. After reconstitution, refrigerate at 2-8C and use within ~4 weeks. Protect from light and avoid repeated freeze-thaw.
contraindications: Active or history of cancer (general caution with growth-derived research peptides)
Competitive athletes subject to anti-doping testing (WADA-prohibited)

interactions.note: Body-composition stacking rationale: CJC-1295 raises endogenous GH/IGF-1 to support lean mass and recovery, while AOD-9604 targets adipocyte lipolysis without adding to the IGF-1 load. Synergy is theoretical/anecdotal for fat-loss goals; no controlled human trial validates the combination and both are non-approved research compounds.

Commonly paired for recomposition: Ipamorelin is a selective GH secretagogue (GH pulse without strong cortisol/prolactin effects) and AOD-9604 adds a direct lipolytic angle. Educational framing only; combined safety and efficacy are unstudied in humans.

Both are marketed for fat reduction, but Tesamorelin robustly raises IGF-1 while AOD-9604 does not; stacking two fat-loss GH-axis compounds compounds cost and monitoring needs without added evidence. Consider IGF-1 and glucose monitoring and avoid redundant fat-loss agents.

references.title: Heffernan et al. The effects of human GH and its lipolytic fragment (AOD9604) on lipid metabolism in obese and beta3-AR knock-out mice (Endocrinology 2001, PubMed)
Heffernan et al. The lipolytic action of AOD9604 (hGH fragment 177-191) in isolated rat adipocytes (Endocrinology 2001, PubMed)
Stier et al. Safety and Tolerability of the Hexadecapeptide AOD9604 in Humans (Journal of Endocrinology and Metabolism 2013)
The effect of AOD9604 on weight loss in obese adults: randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled multicenter study (ResearchGate)
Moré et al. Safety and Metabolism of AOD9604, a Novel Nutraceutical Ingredient for Improved Metabolic Health (Journal of Endocrinology and Metabolism)
